---
title: "04-Discussion"
Author: "Mikkel E. Steentoft"
format: html
editor: visual
theme: cosmo
bibliography: references.bib
csl: acta-diabetologica.csl
---

In this study, we used k-means clustering to identify three subgroups with distinct responses on glucose, insulin, GLP-1, GIP and glucagon derived from the three point oral glucose tolerance test. Importantly, the clustering revealed phenotypes not only with different glycemic status, but also distinct incretin and glucagon dynamics, suggesting that postprandial regulation is heterogeneous beyond glucose alone.

Cluster 3 was the most metabolically compromised and adipose subgroup, with insulin resistance and compensatory hyperinsulinemia, dysglycemia, highest VAT, BMI and body fat percentage. This group was also the oldest and had the highest proportion of smokers and individuals with type 2 diabetes, IFG+IGT and IGT. Cluster 3 had elevated fasting insulin levels (53.6 pmol/L) and a 2-hour insulin level of 387.2 pmol/L, alongside the highest 2hPG of 9.1 mmol/L. This indicates that insulin resistance has led to compensatory hyperinsulinemia, suggesting some degree of preserved beta-cell function, but that the hypersecretion of insulin is not sufficient to compensate entirely for the insulin resistance [@defronzo2004]. Glucagon regulation was also impaired in cluster 3, with an early (0-30 minutes) increase in glucagon, despite an adequate late glucagon suppression. Low or negative glucagon suppression results in continued hepatic glucose output in the early postprandial period, contributing to the elevated glucose levels. This is consistent with prior evidence linking impaired early glucagon suppression to insulin resistance and progression to type 2 diabetes [@færch2016; @wagner2017; @knop2007]. Importantly, cluster 3 had almost similar overall glucagon suppression as cluster 1, despite having very different early and late patterns, suggesting that overall suppression alone is not sufficient to assess glucagon regulation, and that the temporal patterns matter. Despite the fact that cluster 2 had the highest insulin sensitivity, this group had lower glucagon suppression at all intervals compared to cluster 1. The finding of nonsuppressed glucagon<sub>120</sub> among insulin-sensitive individuals is in line with previous findings [@færch2016; @wagner2017], which suggests that cluster 2's low glucagon suppression<sub>120</sub> reflects a metabolically healthy phenotype rather than a pathological one. Interestingly, cluster 1 and 2 had relatively similar insulin sensitivity and proportions of individuals with NGT, but very different incretin secretion, with cluster 1 having the lowest incretin secretion across all clusters. While incretins are typically important regulators of both insulin and glucagon secretion, these mechanisms are glucose-dependent [@christensen2011], indicating that the preserved insulin sensitivity can maintain adequate glucagon regulation even when incretin secretion is reduced. However, the lower incretin secretion in cluster 1 also raises the question of whether this reflects a subgroup at higher risk of developing type 2 diabetes, as lower levels of GLP-1 previously has been suggested to already be present in individuals with prediabetes [@færch2015].
